TextENGNew: ``You don't need to remarry.``
That was the first thing out of my mouth when I got home, so Dad looked blindsided.
The undertone in my voice told him I hadn't approved, and a look of disappointment crossed his face.
``...I understand.@k@vS09/00/172900020. ...I know how you feel, Reina.@k@vS09/00/172900021. I should have talked it over with you beforehand—``
``Just cancel the proposal. That woman is nothing.``
``Don't call her 'that woman.'@k@vS09/00/172900023. Rina-san is...``
``I'll tell you the truth.@k@vS09/02/170200353. That woman and a man called Teppei are a pair of criminals.@k@vS09/02/170200354. She only got close to you for our money.``
changeReason: The original translates 鉄 as 'Tetsu' but the new translation uses 'Teppei' (鉄平), the character's full name; while 鉄 could be either a nickname or abbreviation, the new version correctly identifies the character.

TextENGNew: Dad, clueless as ever, seemed more offended by me calling her "that woman" than anything else.
I could have chosen my words more carefully, perhaps, but I'd communicated the truth succinctly.
I told him everything I'd seen and overheard at the coffee shop.
...As expected, Dad fired back in Rina's defense.
I was disappointed, yet at the same time, a strange weight lifted from my shoulders—I now understood there had truly been no solution other than killing Rina.
Apparently deciding that my consent was essential for the remarriage, Dad reined in his anger on his own and pasted on a conciliatory smile.
``Come on, Reina... let's all sit down and talk this through.@k@vS09/00/172900025. I'm sure you'll understand once you hear us out.``
...Even when the owner dies, a pet is still a pet.
Fine.@k I'd opened the gate all the same.
...Even a pet eventually realizes it's no longer captive.
That wasn't something I could teach him.@k Dad had to figure it out himself.
Unable to bear Dad's tame, excuse-laden smile, I left the living room.
Time had healed the pain of Mom's divorce.
...Just as dripping water wears through stone, time would eventually heal any wound.
I'd done what I could.@k Now I just had to wait.
But the day I'd been waiting for arrived far sooner than I'd imagined.
Late that very next night.@k We had a visitor.
It was the man called Teppei.
changeReason: Same as MID 84361: the original uses 'Tetsu' but the character is Teppei (鉄平); the new translation uses the correct full name.

TextENGNew: Teppei Houjou.
He didn't knock—he smashed through the storm shutters to break in, giving us both a terrible fright.
changeReason: The original says Teppei 'smashed the window,' but the Japanese 鎧戸 means 'storm shutters' — a different point of entry that also characterizes his brute force differently.

TextENGNew: At first I stiffened, thinking he'd come about Rina's disappearance, but that wasn't it at all.
Teppei's visit was based on... the plan he and Rina had arranged beforehand.
It was the finale of their badger game.
Originally, Dad and Rina were probably supposed to be in bed together at this hour tonight.
Teppei had wanted to burst in on exactly that scene.
He searched the house roughly but couldn't find Rina anywhere.
Even so, unfazed, he seized Dad by the collar and rattled off threats with practiced ease.
changeReason: The original says Teppei was 'yelling threats,' but the Japanese 実に慣れた口調で emphasizes he was delivering threats with well-practiced ease — suggesting this is routine for him, not mere rage.

TextENGNew: Teppei produced documents proving his legal marriage to Rina, then berated Dad, demanding to know how he'd take responsibility for seducing another man's wife.
Dad apparently had no idea the two were married. It hit him like a thunderbolt.
On top of that, Dad was small and timid by nature.
From start to finish, he just let Teppei bully and browbeat him without resistance.
I watched it all from a distance, feeling strangely detached.
...The more Teppei raged, the faster Dad's delusion would crumble.
Yes—this was medicine for him.
The festering wound I'd been hoping time would heal—this man had lanced it clean in under an hour.
changeReason: The original uses 'cancer' but the Japanese 膿んだ患部 means 'festering wound/infected area' — a different medical metaphor; also 'depend on time' vs 'hoping time would heal' captures Rena's passive approach better in the new version.

TextENGNew: Once I confirmed that Dad had finally been shocked awake from his dream, I stepped in belatedly and shielded him.
changeReason: The original's 'waited to help' misses the nuances: 今さらのように (as if belatedly/after the fact) suggests calculated timing, and 割って入り...庇う (intervened and shielded) describes physical action, not just 'helping.'

TextENGNew: I told Teppei I had something important to discuss, and pushed him outside.
``So,@k@vS09/21/172000066. whaddya want?``
``Has Rina-san... come back to Okinomiya since yesterday?``
``The truth is, Rina-san is... hiding. There are circumstances.``
``Rina-san told me to bring you to her when you showed up at our house.``
``...Hah??@k@vS09/21/172000071. The hell is Ritsuko thinkin'...?``
Teppei followed me without a moment's hesitation.
Through the pitch dark, guided by the single beam of a flashlight.@k To the place no one visits, no one hears, and everyone has forgotten.
He peppered me with questions along the way, but I had nothing to tell him.
I deflected with a meaningful "Please ask Rina-san yourself."
changeReason: The original omits 意味ありげに (meaningfully/suggestively) — Rena's response was deliberately cryptic, not a simple redirect.

TextENGNew: That apparently made him think Rina had turned traitor—that she was seriously considering marriage for real.
Snorting with rage, he trotted after me without the faintest trace of caution.
I'd known a day like this would come soon and had prepared accordingly.@k When we reached the spot I'd chosen, I abruptly switched off the flashlight.
Anyone would panic if the only light in the darkness were snatched away.
...But this was my domain.
I didn't need light.
If anything, I could sense my surroundings more vividly with my eyes closed.
I hoisted the heavy axe I'd laid in the grass.
changeReason: The original translates 大斧 as 'big hatchet,' but 大斧 means 'large axe' — a significantly larger and more dangerous weapon than a hatchet.

TextENGNew: ``Sorry, I think the battery connection is loose...@k@vS09/02/170200359. Just a moment...``
``'Kay. Make it quick.``
My voice came out small and helpless.@k But my arms had already raised the brutal, heavy axe high overhead.
changeReason: Old adds 'purposely' not in Japanese, and says 'hatchet' instead of 'axe' (大斧).

TextENGNew: ``...Oh wait, I got a lighter.@k@vS09/21/172000076. Lemme just flick this on...``
Teppei fished a lighter from his pocket and struck a flame.
And in that meager glow, the sight before him was probably beyond comprehension.
A girl, standing right in front of him, axe raised to strike.
Teppei's eyes flew wide.
Even without understanding what was happening, he grasped that his life was in mortal danger.
But comprehension didn't translate to reaction fast enough.
And even if his brain had caught up—even if he'd shielded his head with both arms—it would have made no difference.
I'd known Teppei would eventually come to our house!
That's why I'd prepared.
I just hadn't expected it to bear fruit that same night.
I'd chosen my weapon with care.
The range, the distance, the exact spot—I'd thought through every detail.
So the outcome was inevitable!
The big man who'd bragged about his fighting prowess at the coffee shop crumpled pathetically before a weapon prepared with killing intent.
He looked like a victim straight out of a third-rate horror movie.
changeReason: The original says 'B movie' but the Japanese specifies 出来の悪い三流ホラー映画 — a 'poorly-made third-rate horror movie,' emphasizing both the horror genre and the low quality.

TextENGNew: I had truly done it alone... I had felled this massive man.
...He must have had plenty of experience with violence.
But he never got the chance to use it.@k He'd never imagined I'd be that prepared.
With this, it was all over.
The fools who had ensnared my father and tried to steal our assets were all gone.
I had done what I could, and I had protected my life.
...If I'd had this courage and resolve when it was Mom's turn, I might have killed Uncle Akihito.
changeReason: The original specifies 'when my mom told me about the divorce,' but the Japanese 母の時にも is intentionally vague ('when it was Mom's turn'), leaving the reader to infer the context.

TextENGNew: Then only one life would have needed to be taken.
Come on, Rena.@k Let's finish everything up.
I already knew what to do next... I'd already prepared.@k Everything, every last detail, arranged in advance.
changeReason: Old adds 'a place to hide his body' not in the Japanese.

TextENGNew: I felt a slickness on my hand. I shone the flashlight on it.
...What I'd feared was blood turned out to be just sweat.
changeReason: Neither translation perfectly matches the Japanese, which says 気持ち悪いと感じたそれ ('what I felt was disgusting') — the original and new both add 'blood' which is inferred but not stated in the source.

TextENGNew: But the sensation bothered me so much that I kept shining the flashlight on my hands, again and again.
I'd done all this... to seize my happiness.
After going this far, if I couldn't be happy—that would be too cruel.
changeReason: Old states 'I should be happy now' but Japanese is a conditional expressing dread: 'if I still can't be happy, that would be too cruel.'

TextENGNew: I'd stained my hands red with sin...
changeReason: The original says 'got blood on my hands' but the Japanese 悪事に手を真っ赤に染めて means 'stained my hands red with sin/evil deeds' — a moral metaphor, not literal blood.

TextENGNew: I wanted God to acknowledge that.
changeReason: The original says 'get credit for that from him' — ambiguous about who 'him' refers to; the Japanese clearly says 神さま (God), and 'acknowledge' is more fitting than 'get credit for.'

TextENGNew: Oyashiro-sama.
The day my family left Hinamizawa and everything started going wrong—I can finally rewind back to that point now, can't I?
changeReason: Old adds 'Let me ask you a question' and omits leaving Hinamizawa.

TextENGNew: I will live in Hinamizawa.
With Dad, from now on, forever.@k Happily.
It was rare for Rena to doze off during class.
She wasn't the type to stay up late and lose sleep.
She was probably exhausted from all the housework and everything else.
Everyone seemed to know that. Nobody tried to scold her for it—not even Chie-sensei.
changeReason: The original says 'nobody tried to wake her up,' but the Japanese 咎める means 'to scold/reproach' — the point is that nobody blamed Rena, not that they didn't wake her.

TextENGNew: She'd wake briefly, then surrender to drowsiness and nod off again.
There's a saying about her swaying being like "rowing a boat"—and I had to admit, it was apt.
changeReason: The original uses 'rowing a boat' as a direct simile, but the Japanese is meta-commentary on the Japanese idiom 船を漕ぐ (to row a boat = to nod off); Keiichi is remarking on how apt the expression is.

TextENGNew: But still... this was class, so maybe I should wake her up...?
I reached out to give her a nudge, but Mion caught my eye and signaled me to stop.
``Let her sleep.@k@vS09/03/170300111. Everyone has days like this.``
...I'd only learned just recently that Rena lived alone with her father.

When she'd invited me over for dinner to thank me for the Randy-kun doll, her mother was nowhere to be seen even late into the evening.@k I'd tried to be considerate, asking if it was tough with both parents working—and later deeply regretted it.
changeReason: Old reverses the direction of the dinner invitation.

TextENGNew: ...Rena had told me her mother wasn't there because of a divorce.@k ...When I quietly asked Mion about it afterward, she'd already known. She confirmed it was true.
...Studying during the day, playing her heart out after school, then going home to handle all the housework in place of her mother.
...Living days like that—had I hurt her feelings without even realizing it...?
``...You didn't know, Kei-chan. It couldn't be helped.@k@vS09/03/170300111_2. But from now on, let's be more mindful.``
If Mion hadn't said that to cheer me up, I think I would have dwelled on it for a long time.
``...I had no idea she'd been carrying that kind of burden.``
``Rena isn't the type to show that side of herself.@k@vS09/03/170300113. Maybe the reason she always acts so cheerful is because she makes a conscious effort to be that way.``
Rena—the one who'd stated so plainly that happy times are finite.
She'd said she knew from experience that happiness could end without warning. What kind of hardships had she been through?@k I couldn't even imagine.
That must be why she tried to wring every last drop of fun from the peaceful days she had.
Calling those days "boring"—I'd surely wounded her.
...Thinking about it made me feel guilty for living comfortably under my parents' roof, both of them healthy and present.
Come to think of it... Satoko and Rika-chan had both lost their parents.
And even Mion lived apart from her parents, studying under her grandmother as an heir-in-training.
When that hit me, I felt... a vague sense of unworthiness.
``There's no reason to feel bad about living with your parents, Kei-chan.``
Mion seemed to have read my mind.@k She said it with one of her rare, composed expressions.
``I think family is probably the first community we're born into as children.@k@vS09/03/170300116. But family isn't the only community there is.``
changeReason: The original says family is 'the most common community,' but the Japanese 一番最初のコミュニティ means 'the very first community' — Mion's point is about the chronological order of belonging, not frequency.

TextENGNew: ``...You mean friends?``
``Now you're getting it.``
``Are we... as important as family to Rena...?``
``Cleaning, laundry, cooking, shopping.@k@vS09/03/170300120. Rena could easily spend her afternoons just napping from exhaustion.``
``But she doesn't.@k@vS09/03/170300122. She spends every free moment after school with us in club.@k@vS09/03/170300123. That means... how should I put it... yeah—she considers us family.``
changeReason: The original translates 仲間 as 'best friends' and the new as 'family'; the Japanese word 仲間 means 'companions/comrades.' Given the scene's context where Mion argues friends equal family, both take liberties but in different directions.

TextENGNew: ``To Rena, we're... friends who matter as much as family?``
Mion blinked at me blankly.
It seemed like we weren't quite connecting.
``Hmm... I don't know what 'friends' meant to you before today, Kei-chan.@k@vS09/03/170300126. ...But aren't friends and family the exact same thing?``
changeReason: The original presents Mion's words as a statement ('I believe friends are just as important as family'), but the Japanese is a rhetorical question (じゃないのかなぁ — 'aren't friends and family the exact same thing?'), reflecting Mion's genuine puzzlement at Keiichi's distinction.

TextENGNew: ``...It is not something to overthink.``
Before I'd noticed, Rika-chan had joined the conversation.
``What matters is having a place you belong.@k@vS09/05/170500027. Whether you call those people 'family' or 'friends'—it's just a difference in words.``
``...To you, Rika-chan, are friends and family the same thing?``
``...I believe so.@k@vS09/05/170500029. And I believe Satoko, Mii, and Rena feel the same way.``
``I guess that's why we always try to be together.``
``Rika-chan, do you ever feel lonely when everyone's around?``
``...Everyone is my family.@k@vS09/05/170500031. How could I feel lonely?``
Mion nodded in agreement.
Satoko was busy chatting with other classmates, but she'd surely nod the same way.
...And Rena too.
``I'm... kind of embarrassed.``

``I've been using the word 'friends' all this time.@k@vS09/01/170100238. ...But I don't think I truly understood what it meant until today.@k@vS09/01/170100239. Friends are... something far more precious and irreplaceable than I realized.``
``...That's... kind of obvious, you know.@k@vS09/03/170300131. If you really didn't get that until today, you'd better engrave it on your heart.``
``Also, maybe don't say that out loud again!``
``What you just said was pretty embarrassing, Kei-chan~``
Mion teased me, as if the moment had already passed.
I think I understood a little better now why Rena always put so much earnest effort into playing and having fun.
And I wanted to be worthy of that—to be the kind of friend who could match her all-out effort.
Rena let out a cute little snore.
Even Chie-sensei couldn't let snoring slide, apparently.
``Class president, please wake Ryuugu-san.``
``Rena, hey, come on.``
``Fwaaahh...``
The drowsy noise Rena made when Mion nudged her got the whole class laughing.
Rena realized the giggles were directed at her, snapped fully awake, and promptly pretended she hadn't been sleeping at all.
The act was so transparent it was hilarious, and I couldn't help bursting out laughing.
Outside the window, even though it was only June, the sky blazed a deep summer blue with huge white clouds billowing across it.
This fine weather wouldn't last forever, either.
It could cloud over and turn into a downpour without warning.
So while the sky was clear, we should play our hearts out.
The cicadas' chorus seemed to mock us for still being stuck in class.@k Ah, damn it—hurry up and let school end already.
``What's up, Keiichi-kun?@k@vS09/02/170200363. You look really happy. Did something happen?``
``Nothing special about today.@k@vS09/01/170100243. Today is today, and tomorrow is tomorrow.``

changeReason: The original translates うん？ as 'Yeah...' (agreement), but with the question mark in the Japanese, it's a questioning sound — 'Mm?' or 'Hm?' — expressing confusion, not agreement.

TextENGNew: ``If the weather's nice today, that's enough.@k@vS09/01/170100245. I don't care about tomorrow's forecast.@k@vS09/01/170100246. I just want to make the most of today—play until I drop.@k@vS09/01/170100247. So thoroughly that I won't have any regrets, no matter when the weather turns.``
That was something Rena had taught me.
That peaceful, ordinary days are the happiest days of all—irreplaceable beyond measure.
``Mion~, what's on the club agenda today?@k@vS09/01/170100249. And I gotta say, the punishments have been a bit tame lately!``
``Heh heh heh!@k@vS09/03/170300136. Big talk, Kei-chan.@k@vS09/03/170300137. If you insist, I'll show you a living hell this afternoon...!``
``K-Keiichi-kun, don't provoke her too much...``

``Hey, what's the matter, Rena? Getting cold feet?@k@vS09/01/170100251. Bring it on!@k@vS09/01/170100252. Let's go wild and show them what we've got!@k@vS09/01/170100253. You and I still have a score to settle, remember?``
I flashed a grin at her, and Rena couldn't help but grin back.
``Ahaha, you're right.@k@vS09/02/170200367. All right—I'm going to give it everything I've got!``
``I still owe Rena-san from the other day, after all!``
``I shall repay you with interest!``
``Now this is what I like to hear.``
``Things are heating up! Heh heh heh!``
``...I wish school would end soon.``
The end of classes couldn't come soon enough.
...But even these moments—everyone fired up and talking together—felt precious and radiant to me now.
``Yeah.@k@vS09/01/170100255. I wish it were after school already.``
